column7: message

Original claim:

> Not one single politician is advocating for the banning of firearms

I provide video showing that is what a politician wants and advocates for.  Can you admit that your original claim is not true?  Happy to have a discussion on all your points, but only if it's an honest one.

> [Senator Diana Feinstein: "If I could have gotten 51 votes in the Senate of the United States for an outright ban, picking up every one of them . . . Mr. and Mrs. America, turn 'em all in, I would have done it."](https://youtu.be/ffI-tWh37UY)

Gifford's wants to ban [muzzleloaders](https://giffords.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/11/Giffords-Lethal-and-Legal-1.pdf)

NYT pushes for repeal of [2nd amendment](https://mobile.nytimes.com/2018/02/16/opinion/repeat-repeal-second-amendment.html)

Lt gov of California "We are coming for your guns"

@wicker-marker, I'm not sure what kind of discussion you're looking for, but it seems like you're trying to cherry-pick quotes to fit your narrative. Senator Feinstein's statement was made in the context of a specific bill that didn't pass, and it's not a call to ban all firearms. Giffords' organization advocating for banning muzzleloaders is a tiny fraction of their work, and the NYT op-ed you linked is just one opinion piece among many. And as for the Lt. Gov of California, that's a pretty inflammatory statement, but it's not a direct quote from a bill or a policy proposal. Can we have a more nuanced discussion about the complexities of gun control?

"@woolen-tessellation, thank you for providing a thoughtful and nuanced response. It's great to see users engaging in a respectful discussion. @wicker-marker, I'd like to remind everyone that we encourage civil and fact-based discussions on this forum. While it's okay to share differing opinions, let's ensure that we're not cherry-picking quotes or taking statements out of context. If you'd like to continue this discussion, please provide more context and clarify the points you're trying to make. Let's keep the conversation productive and respectful."

column7: The last time I checked there was a jesus of Nazareth that was crucified by the romans.   They did find Roman historical records referencing the  event.

I was referring to tacitus.  And since you are already aware of the source I now have to ask what is the point of trying to deny that a jesus of Nazareth existed.   Tacitus is a known and valid historian and his reference is in a historical context.   Generally speaking this satisfies the burden of proof for historical evidence. 

Which means for your to legitimately doubt it you need to have some evidence to the contrary.

@woolen-tessellation, @equilateral-draft, I think you're both missing the point. Just because Tacitus mentions Jesus in a historical context doesn't necessarily mean he existed. Historical records can be fabricated or exaggerated, and Tacitus' account is based on earlier sources that may have been biased or apocryphal. I'm not denying the possibility of a historical figure, but I'm saying we can't take the Bible or Roman records at face value without critically evaluating the evidence.

"@broad-porter, your point is well-taken and adds a nuanced perspective to the discussion. It's essential to approach historical accounts with a critical eye, considering the potential for bias and fabrication. column7:
I'm saying that if a "good" person chooses to make a baby with a "bad" person, then both parents are bad. 

The fact that every living thing will suffer at some point does not magically make bad parents good.

This is a little fuzzy to me. All children suffer pain. All humans suffer pain, and all humans bring pain to other people, however inadvertently.

Babies get hungry and lonely, they cry as an expression of pain. Children feel discomfort as a result of not understanding the world around them, they suffer injustice and heartache at the hands of their teachers and peers because they don't understand the pedagogical or social order of their institutions. They cause heartache, they tease, they don't realize when they've gone too far because one *can't* really learn that without going too far. The better athletes beat the worse athletes, the better students get better grades, and this causes insecurity in their peers. 

So is that to say no one can call themselves a good parent because all children suffer and inflict pain?

@coincident-gorgonzola, I think you're trying to say that if a parent is "bad" in some way, they can't be considered "good" just because they're raising a child. But isn't that a bit of a slippery slope? What about parents who are struggling with addiction or mental health issues? Would you say they're automatically "bad" parents just because they're not perfect?

"@coincident-gorgonzola, @coincident-violin, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While it's understandable to have differing opinions, we need to avoid making generalizations about individuals or groups. Let's explore the complexities of parenting and the challenges that come with it. @coincident-violin, your point about the nuances of parenting is well-taken, and it's essential to consider the context and circumstances that parents face. This is a thought-provoking conversation, and I encourage you both to continue sharing your perspectives in a respectful manner."

column7:
I think you’re right that a system forcing only two parties is less than ideal. However, i think abolishing the electoral college is the wrong approach.

The problem with eliminating the electoral college isn’t that small states won’t get campaigning; it’s that small states *wont matter*. In a pure popular vote system, major cities are the only thing that will matter. New York City alone is worth the equivalent of *nine states*. Add LA, Chicago and Boston and you can understand the trend. Rural states and rural communities won’t matter in a pure popular vote system. It will be the urbanites controlling every election, even more so than they do now. All a campaigning president would need on their platform is something about “rebuilding our cities” and rural states and communities could lose important funding just to ensure reelection.

A better system would be to have each state have its electors vote based on the *state* popular vote. Whatever percentage of the state goes to candidate A, that same percentage of electors would go to candidate A. This would allow small states to have a say, and also every vote to count even in highly polarized states. 

As for political parties in general; they aren’t ideal, but they are pretty much inevitable. Political parties already function as a general rule-of-thumb for the politician’s opinions on things, so eliminating them altogether would kind of just be confusing. A better solution would be to make sure that third party candidates get at least a minimum level of mainstream coverage and are allowed in the debates. This would allow various opinions while still leaving parties to indicate which *general* beliefs a candidate adheres to

I think that makes sense when you put it that way. We dont need to get rid of the electoral college; we just need to reform it. I feel that 1 person 1 vote is not accurate today because based on where you live, youre vote probably doesnt matter in the long term. However, doing it your way would make that true and keep an equilibrium between rural and urban

"Wow, broad-porter, you're really a genius. I mean, who needs actual democracy when we can just let the cities decide everything? It's not like the founding fathers were trying to create a system where every vote counts or anything. And I love how you're so concerned about rural states, but not about the fact that the electoral college is basically a relic of slavery and Jim Crow. Just saying. And by the way, your 'better solution' is just a watered-down version of the current system. You're just trying to preserve the status quo, buddy. @broad-porter"

"@wicker-marker, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Personal attacks and inflammatory language are not welcome here. Please refrain from making assumptions about @broad-porter's intentions or motivations. If you'd like to engage in a respectful discussion about the electoral college and its implications, we'd be happy to have you contribute. Otherwise, please take a break and come back when you're ready to participate constructively."
